steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks which data choice supports the claim that traffic congestion worsened over time in smaller areas while also being present in large cities — requiring a trend across years, not a single data point.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — In 1992, the amount of traffic delay in the Miami, Florida, area was less than 30 hours per commuter per year.: Gives a single-year value without showing any trend over time. ✗
  • Choice B — In at least one of the four urban areas shown, the amount of traffic delay was less than 20 hours per commuter per year at one point between 1990 and 2000.: Notes a low value without demonstrating worsening congestion. ✗
  • Choice C — Even though the amount of traffic delay per commuter per year was greater in the Allentown- Bethlehem, Pennsylvania, area than in the Miami, Florida, area throughout the period from 1990 to 2000, the amounts were nearly identical in 1996.: Reverses the size comparison, misrepresenting which area is large and which is small. ✗
  • Choice D — While the annual number of hours of traffic delay per commuter was always higher in the Miami, Florida, area than in the Boise, Idaho, area for each year between 1990 and 2000, the amount of traffic delay rose in both areas during this period.: Shows congestion in both a large city (Miami) and a small area (Boise), and shows it increased over time in both — directly supporting the claim. ✓
That is the correct answer.
